summary: Reporter observes missing closing </svg> tag when using SKSvgCanvas with SKManagedWStream on SkiaSharp 3.119.0. The root cause is disposal ordering: `using var canvas` defers disposal to end-of-scope, so `managedStream.ToArray()` reads the stream before the canvas destructor writes the closing tag. This is by-design behavior in Skia — the SkSVGDevice destructor writes the closing </svg> tag.

stepsToReproduce: Create MemoryStream, SKManagedWStream, and SKSvgCanvas using 'using var' declarations
Call managedStream.ToArray() before the canvas is disposed (still in same scope)
Observe the resulting SVG is missing the closing </svg> tag
reproEvidence.environmentDetails: SkiaSharp 3.119.0, Blazor + iOS via Rider, web release builds

screenshots.description: SVG renders correctly when closing tag manually added

versionAnalysis.relevanceReason: The behavior is by design in Skia — the SVG device destructor writes the closing tag. This has always been the case. The reporter likely had different disposal ordering in 2.88.x code.

regression.reason: The closing tag has always been written by the SkSVGDevice destructor. The reporter's previous code likely used block-scoped 'using' which ensured disposal before reading the stream. The switch to 'using var' declaration syntax changed the disposal timing.

analysis.summary: The closing </svg> tag is written by the SkSVGDevice C++ destructor, which runs when the SKCanvas is disposed. The reporter uses 'using var canvas = SKSvgCanvas.Create(...)' which defers disposal to end-of-scope, then reads the stream with 'managedStream.ToArray()' before the canvas is disposed. The existing test (SKCanvasTest.SvgCanvasSavesFile) demonstrates the correct pattern: block-scoped 'using' ensures the canvas is disposed before reading the stream.
analysis.rationale: Classified as type/bug because the reporter describes behavior that doesn't match expectations, but the root cause is a usage pattern issue (using var scope), not a defect. The behavior is by-design in Skia — SkSVGDevice::~SkSVGDevice() triggers RAII cleanup of the root AutoElement which writes the closing </svg>. Severity is low because there's a simple workaround (change using var to block-scoped using). The area is area/SkiaSharp since the core issue is SKSvgCanvas usage, and backend/SVG is applicable.

keySignals.text: using var canvas = SKSvgCanvas.Create(bounds, stream); ... result.Data = managedStream.ToArray();
keySignals.interpretation: 'using var' defers disposal to end of enclosing scope. ToArray() is called before canvas disposal, so the closing </svg> tag hasn't been written yet.

keySignals.text: // The root <svg> tag gets closed by the destructor.
keySignals.interpretation: Skia explicitly documents that the closing tag is written by the destructor — this is by-design behavior.

keySignals.text: SKFileWStream seems to output a correct SVG with close tags
keySignals.interpretation: SKFileWStream flushes on each write operation to disk, so the closing tag appears when the destructor runs and file is read afterward. With SKManagedWStream wrapping MemoryStream, reading before disposal means incomplete data.

keySignals.text: using (var svg = SKSvgCanvas.Create(SKRect.Create(100, 100), stream)) { ... } stream.Position = 0; reader.ReadToEnd();
keySignals.interpretation: Existing test demonstrates correct pattern: block-scoped using ensures canvas disposal before reading stream.

codeInvestigation.finding: Line 750 comments: 'The root <svg> tag gets closed by the destructor.' The fRootElement (AutoElement) RAII wrapper writes the closing tag when the SkSVGDevice destructor runs at line 759.

SKSvgCanvas.Create wraps the stream and returns an SKCanvas. The stream overload creates an SKManagedWStream internally and attaches ownership via SKObject.Owned. The SKWStream overload uses SKObject.Referenced to keep the stream alive.
Test SvgCanvasSavesFile uses block-scoped 'using (var svg = SKSvgCanvas.Create(...))' and reads the stream AFTER the using block ends, confirming that disposal must occur before reading.
C API sk_svgcanvas_create_with_stream calls SkSVGCanvas::Make and releases the unique_ptr, transferring ownership to the caller (SKCanvas). Canvas disposal triggers the C++ destructor chain.

workarounds: Use block-scoped 'using (var canvas = SKSvgCanvas.Create(...)) { }' and read the stream after the block ends
Explicitly call canvas.Dispose() before reading the stream if using 'using var' syntax

resolution.hypothesis: The reporter's 'using var' declaration defers canvas disposal to end-of-scope, causing the stream to be read before the SkSVGDevice destructor writes the closing </svg> tag.

proposals.title: Use block-scoped using
proposals.description: Replace 'using var canvas' with a block-scoped 'using' statement so the canvas is disposed before reading the stream.

proposals.title: Explicit Dispose before reading
proposals.description: Keep 'using var' but explicitly dispose the canvas before reading the stream.

resolution.recommendedReason: Idiomatic C# pattern that matches the existing test code. Ensures disposal ordering is clear and avoids double-dispose risk from calling Dispose() explicitly on a 'using var' variable.

actionability.reason: The closing </svg> tag is written by the Skia C++ destructor by design. The issue is disposal ordering in the reporter's code. The existing test demonstrates the correct pattern.

actions.description: Apply SVG backend label and status/by-design
Close as by-design with workaround provided
